skill.name: daily-meeting-update
skill.description: Prepare daily standup updates through an interview-first workflow. The skill can optionally use approved GitHub, Git, Jira, and Claude Code history context.
skill.summary: Build concise daily standup updates from interview answers and approved activity context.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto indicators. Most command and crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown examples or names, but the skill does read local Claude Code history and may use local GitHub, Git, and Jira tools after consent. No prompt injection, malicious persistence, credential theft, or network exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Reads Local Claude Code Session History
medium_findings.description: The skill can run a Python helper that reads JSONL files under ~/.claude/projects. The helper outputs session titles, project paths, branch names, touched files, and command counts. This is useful for standups, but it may expose sensitive local work context if the user approves it without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly locates ~/.claude/projects and parses JSONL session files. The skill asks for consent first, so this is privacy-sensitive rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Uses Local Developer Integrations After Consent
medium_findings.description: The skill detects GitHub CLI, Git, Jira CLI, and Atlassian MCP availability, then asks before pulling activity. These integrations can reveal repository names, PRs, reviews, tickets, and project context. The consent model lowers risk, but the behavior should be visible to users before publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly describe local integration detection and approved data pulls. No implementation of unsafe command construction was found, but the integrations handle potentially sensitive work data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command alerts point to Markdown fenced examples, inline command names, or sample conversations. They are not executable Ruby backtick calls inside a Ruby source file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown code fences and examples. There is no Ruby file or shell interpolation behavior at those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are example GitHub pull request links shown in sample output. No HTTP client, network request, webhook, or upload path was found in the reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are inside output examples and use placeholder org/repo paths. The helper script has no network library usage in the reviewed evidence.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto locations do not contain hashing, encryption, or signature verification code. They appear to be triggered by words such as digest or unrelated Markdown content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations contain descriptions, argparse text, or README tables. No cryptographic algorithm call was found at the cited lines.

low_findings.title: Tool Detection Is Limited Local Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The skill checks for local tool availability and repository context to tailor the workflow. This is expected for the stated purpose, but users should know detection occurs before integration prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local checks are explicitly documented and align with the integration workflow. No evidence suggests the detected environment details are sent elsewhere.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Hidden Directory Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper targets ~/.claude/projects, a hidden directory that may contain sensitive AI session history. Access is presented as optional, but the data should be treated as private.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both the skill instructions and script directly reference the hidden Claude projects directory. The behavior is real, although it supports the stated feature.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Command-Based Integration Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow can use Bash and local developer tools to inspect GitHub, Git, Jira, and Claude Code context. This increases privacy exposure if a user approves broad repositories or projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented workflow names Bash and local CLIs as tools for detection and data pulls. No unsafe shell concatenation was found in the Python helper.

content.value_statement: Daily updates are hard when work is spread across commits, tickets, reviews, and AI sessions. This skill interviews you and turns approved activity context into a concise Markdown standup update.
actual_capabilities: Guides a four-question interview about yesterday, today, blockers, and discussion topics.
Optionally detects GitHub, Git, Jira, Atlassian MCP, and Claude Code history availability.
Asks for approval before pulling activity from supported developer integrations.
Summarizes selected Claude Code sessions using the included Python digest script.
Combines interview answers and approved activity context into a Markdown daily update.
Encourages concise output suitable for a short team standup.
limitations: Integration data depends on locally configured tools and permissions.
Claude Code history can include sensitive local work context and should be reviewed before sharing.
The skill does not verify whether ticket or pull request summaries are complete.
Manual input is still needed for priorities, blockers, and discussion context.

use_cases.title: Prepare a developer standup
use_cases.description: Collect recent work, today plans, blockers, and discussion topics into a short update.

use_cases.title: Recover work context from tools
use_cases.description: Use approved GitHub, Jira, Git, or Claude Code context to remember work from the last day.

use_cases.title: Create consistent team updates
use_cases.description: Generate a repeatable Markdown format for daily syncs across projects and repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Standup
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want to answer manually.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare my daily standup update. Ask me the four standard questions first.

prompt_templates.title: Interview With Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want a guided conversation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare my standup update and show any approved activity context before asking follow-up questions.

prompt_templates.title: GitHub And Jira Update
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when work is tracked across repositories and tickets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare my daily update. Ask which GitHub repositories and Jira projects I approve before pulling activity.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Multi-Source Update
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want a complete but concise update.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare my daily from approved GitHub, Jira, Git, and Claude Code context. Let me select sessions and keep the final update under 15 bullets.

output_examples.input: Prepare my standup update from manual answers.
output_examples.output: A Markdown update with Yesterday, Today, Blockers, and Topics for Discussion sections.

output_examples.input: Use approved GitHub and Jira context for my daily.
output_examples.output: A concise update that includes summarized pull requests, reviews, tickets, plans, and blockers.

output_examples.input: Include selected Claude Code sessions from yesterday.
output_examples.output: A standup note that includes selected session summaries while excluding unselected personal or unrelated work.

best_practices: Review detected activity before it is included in the final update.
Provide context for ticket numbers, pull requests, and technical decisions.
Keep the final update short enough for a daily meeting.
anti_patterns: Do not pull from repositories, tickets, or Claude Code history without explicit approval.
Do not rely only on tool data when blockers or priorities need human context.
Do not share raw session details that include sensitive or unrelated work.

faq.question: Does this skill work without integrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It can prepare an update from manual answers alone.

faq.question: What integrations can it use?
faq.answer: It can use approved GitHub, Git, Jira, Atlassian MCP, and Claude Code history context.

faq.question: Does it access Claude Code history automatically?
faq.answer: The workflow says to ask before running the digest script and to skip it if the user declines.

faq.question: What does the digest script output?
faq.answer: It outputs session titles, project paths, branch names, touched file names, and command counts.

faq.question: Can I exclude personal projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow asks users to select relevant Claude Code sessions before inclusion.

faq.question: What format does it generate?
faq.answer: It generates a Markdown daily update with sections for work, plans, blockers, and discussion topics.
